dante: nel mezzo del cammin di nostra vita mi ritrovai per una selva oscura ché la diritta via era smarrita
singleton: midway in the journey of our life i found myself in a dark wood for the straight way was lost
musa: midway along the journey of our life i woke to find myself in a dark wood for i had wandered off from the straight path
kirkpatrick: at one point midway on our path in life i came around and found myself now searching through a dark wood the right way blurred and lost
durling: in the middle of the journey of our life i came to myself in a dark wood for the straight way was lost

dante: ahi quanto a dir qual era è cosa dura esta selva selvaggia e aspra e forte che nel pensier rinova la paura!
singleton: ah how hard it is to tell what that wood was wild rugged harsh; the very thought of it renews the fear!
musa: how hard it is to tell what it was like this wood of wilderness savage and stubborn (the thought of it brings back all my old fears)
kirkpatrick: how hard it is to say what that wood was a wilderness savage brute harsh and wild only to think of it renews my fear!
durling: ah how hard a thing it is to say what that wood was so savage and harsh and strong that the thought of it renews my fear!

dante: tant' è amara che poco è più morte; ma per trattar del ben ch'i' vi trovai dirò de l'altre cose ch'i' v'ho scorte
singleton: it is so bitter that death is hardly more so but to treat of the good that i found in it i will tell of the other things i saw there
musa: a bitter place! death could scarce be bitterer but if i would show the good that came of it i must talk about things other than the good
kirkpatrick: so bitter that thought that death is hardly more so but since my theme will be the good i found there i mean to speak of other things i saw
durling: it is so bitter that death is little more so! but to treat of the good that i found there i will tell of the other things i saw

dante: io non so ben ridir com' i' v'intrai tant' era pien di sonno a quel punto che la verace via abbandonai
singleton: i cannot rightly say how i entered it i was so full of sleep at the moment i left the true way;
musa: how i entered there i cannot truly say i had become so sleepy at the moment when i first strayed leaving the path of truth;
kirkpatrick: i do not know i cannot rightly say how first i came to be here – so full of sleep that moment abandoning the true way on
durling: i cannot really say how i entered there so full of sleep was i at the point when i abandoned the true way

dante: ma poi ch'i' fui al piè d'un colle giunto là dove terminava quella valle che m'avea di paura il cor compunto
singleton: but when i had reached the foot of a hill there at the end of the valley that had pierced my heart with fear
musa: but when i found myself at the foot of a hill at the edge of the wood’s beginning down in the valley where i first felt my heart plunged deep in fear
kirkpatrick: but then on reaching the foot of a hill which marked the limit of the dark ravine that had before so pierced my heart with panic
durling: but when i had reached the foot of a hill where the valley ended that had pierced my heart with fear

dante: guardai in alto e vidi le sue spalle vestite già de' raggi del pianeta che mena dritto altrui per ogne calle

musa: i raised my head and saw the hilltop shawled in morning rays of light sent from the planet that leads men straight ahead on every road
kirkpatrick: i looked to that height and saw its shoulders already clothed in rays from the planet that leads all others on any road aright
durling: i looked on high and saw its shoulders clothed already with the rays of the planet that leads us straight on every path

dante: allor fu la paura un poco queta che nel lago del cor m'era durata la notte ch'i' passai con tanta pieta
singleton: then the fear was somewhat quieted that had continued in the lake of my heart through the night i had passed so piteously
musa: and then only did terror start subsiding in my heart’s lake which rose to heights of fear that night i spent in deepest desperation
kirkpatrick: my fears at this were somewhat quieted though terror awash in the lake of my heart had lasted all the night i’d passed in anguish
durling: then was the fear a little quieted that in the lake of my heart had lasted through the night i passed with so much anguish

dante: e come quei che con lena affannata uscito fuor del pelago a la riva si volge a l'acqua perigliosa e guata
singleton: and as he who with laboring breath has escaped from the deep to the shore turns to look back on the dangerous waters
musa: just as a swimmer still with panting breath now safe upon the shore out of the deep might turn for one last look at the dangerous waters
kirkpatrick: and then like someone labouring for breath who safely reaching shore from open sea still turns and stares across those perilous waves
durling: and like one with laboring breath come forth out of the deep onto the shore who turns back to the perilous water and stares:

dante: così l'animo mio ch'ancor fuggiva si volse a retro a rimirar lo passo che non lasciò già mai persona viva
singleton: so my mind which was still fleeing turned back to gaze upon the pass that never left anyone alive
musa: so i although my mind was turned to flee turned round to gaze once more upon the pass that never let a living soul escape
kirkpatrick: so in my mind – my thoughts all fleeing still – i turned around to marvel at that strait that let no living soul pass through till now
durling: so my spirit still fleeing turned back to gaze again at the pass that has never yet left anyone alive

dante: poi ch'èi posato un poco il corpo lasso ripresi via per la piaggia diserta sì che 'l piè fermo sempre era 'l più basso

musa: i rested my tired body there awhile and then began to climb the barren slope (i dragged my stronger foot and limped along)
kirkpatrick: and then – my weary limbs a little rested – i started up the lonely scree once more the foot that drives me always set the lower
durling: after i had a little rested my weary body i took my way again along that deserted slope so that my halted foot was always the lower

dante: ed ecco quasi al cominciar de l'erta una lonza leggiera e presta molto che di pel macolato era coverta;

musa: beyond the point the slope begins to rise sprang up a leopard trim and very swift! it was covered by a pelt of many spots
kirkpatrick: but look now! almost as the scarp begins a leopard light and lively svelte and quick its coat displaying a dappled marking
durling: and behold almost at the beginning of the steep a leopard light and very swift covered with spotted fur;

dante: e non mi si partia dinanzi al volto anzi 'mpediva tanto il mio cammino ch'i' fui per ritornar più volte vòlto
singleton: and it did not depart from before my eyes but did so im- pede my way that more than once i turned round to go back
musa: and everywhere i looked the beast was there blocking my way so time and time again i was about to turn and go back down
kirkpatrick: this never ceased to dance before my face no on it came so bothering my tread i’d half a mind at every turn to turn
durling: and it did not depart from before my face but rather so impeded my way that i was at several turns turned to go back

musa: the hour was early in the morning then the sun was climbing up with those same stars that had accompanied it on the world’s first day the day divine love
kirkpatrick: the time however was the hour of dawn the sun was mounting and those springtime stars that rose along with it when holy love
durling: the time was the beginning of the morning and the sun was mounting up with those stars that were with it when god’s love

dante: mosse di prima quelle cose belle  sì ch'a bene sperar m'era cagione di quella fiera a la gaetta pelle
singleton: first set those beautiful things in motion so that the hour of the day and the sweet season gave me cause for good hope of that beast with the gay skin;
musa: set their beauty turning; so the hour and sweet season of creation encouraged me to think i could get past
kirkpatrick: first moved to being all these lovely things so these – the morning hour the gentle season – led me to find good reason for my hopes
durling: first set those lovely things in motion; so that i took reason to have good hope of that beast with its gaily painted hide

dante: l'ora del tempo e la dolce stagione  ma non sì che paura non mi desse la vista che m'apparve d'un leone
singleton: yet not so much that i did not feel afraid at the sight of a lion
musa: that gaudy beast wild in its spotted pelt but then good hope gave way and fear returned when the figure of a lion loomed up before me
kirkpatrick: seeing that creature with its sparkling hide yet not so far that no fear pressed on me to see appearing now a lion face
durling: from the hour of the morning and the sweet season; but not so that i did not fear the sight of a lion that appeared to me

dante: questi parea che contra me venisse con la test' alta e con rabbiosa fame sì che parea che l'aere ne tremesse
singleton: that appeared to me and seemed to be coming at me head high and raging with hunger so that the air seemed to tremble at it;
musa: and he was coming straight toward me it seemed with head raised high and furious with hunger —the air around him seemed to fear his presence
kirkpatrick: this as it seemed came on and on towards me hungrily its ravening head held high so that in dread the air around it trembled
durling: he appeared to be coming against me with his head high and with raging hunger so that the air appeared to tremble at him

dante: ed una lupa che di tutte brame sembiava carca ne la sua magrezza e molte genti fé già viver grame

musa: and now a she-wolf came that in her leanness seemed racked with every kind of greediness (how many people she has brought to grief!)
kirkpatrick: and then a wolf and she who seemingly was gaunt yet gorged on every kind of craving – and had already blighted many a life –
durling: and a she-wolf that seemed laden with all cravings in her leanness and has caused many peoples to live in wretchedness

dante: questa mi porse tanto di gravezza con la paura ch'uscia di sua vista ch'io perdei la speranza de l'altezza
singleton: she put such heaviness upon me with the fear that came from sight of her that i lost hope of the height
musa: this last beast brought my spirit down so low with fear that seized me at the sight of her i lost all hope of going up the hill
kirkpatrick: so heavily oppressed my thought with fears which spurted even at the sight of her i lost all hope of reaching to those heights
durling: she put on me so much heaviness with the fear that came from the sight of her that i lost hope of reaching the heights

dante: e qual è quei che volontieri acquista e giugne 'l tempo che perder lo face che 'n tutti suoi pensier piange e s'attrista;
singleton: and like one who is eager in winning but when the time comes that makes him lose weeps and is saddened in all his thoughts

musa: while i was rushing down to that low place my eyes made out a figure coming toward me of one grown faint perhaps from too much silence

durling: he replied: “not a man i was formerly a man and my parents were lombards mantuans both by birth
